question.text: The 'high watermark principle' in PMS fee calculation ensures that:

answer.text: Fees are always calculated on the highest NAV achieved
Profit sharing fees are not charged until the portfolio exceeds the previous high value on which fees were paid
The PMS manager receives a fixed minimum fee regardless of performance
The investor pays fees only on returns above the risk-free rate

explanation.text: **The Logic:** High watermark is the corpus value/NAV at which performance fees were last paid. If the portfolio falls below this level, no profit-sharing fees are charged until it crosses the previous high. Example: If fees paid at Rs. 60L, and portfolio drops to Rs. 55L, no performance fee until it exceeds Rs. 60L again. **The Trap:** The hurdle rate (not high watermark) relates to minimum return before performance fees kick in — they are separate concepts. **Cross-Exam Context:** PMS fee structures are tested in X-A — understanding fee mechanics is essential for advisory. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** High watermark protects clients from paying performance fees to recover from losses the PMS manager caused — always verify this is in the agreement before recommending a PMS.
